question: You are at your best friend's birthday party but you feel left out even though you were invited. What is that feeling telling you?
option_a: That you do not belong there
option_b: That something feels off and it is worth noticing
option_c: That you should go home
option_d: That you are being silly
explanation: This happens anywhere â€” at a party, walking into a new sports club, sitting in a classroom where everyone already knows each other. The feeling does not mean you do not belong. It just means something feels off and it is worth noticing rather than pushing away.
hint: Does the feeling mean something is wrong or just that something feels different?

question: Your teacher reads out your gold star in front of the class. Everyone claps. You feel embarrassed instead of happy. Is that ok?
option_a: No â€” you should feel happy when you get praised
option_b: Yes â€” people feel things differently and embarrassment when being watched is very normal
option_c: No â€” it means you do not like your teacher
option_d: Yes â€” it means you did not deserve it
explanation: Being watched can feel uncomfortable even when something good is happening. This comes up at prize givings, sports presentations, being picked out in assembly. Your feelings are being honest â€” not wrong.
hint: Can a good moment feel uncomfortable at the same time?

question: Your whole class is excited about sports day. You are dreading it. You have not told anyone. What is the best thing to do with that feeling?
option_a: Pretend to be excited so nobody notices
option_b: Tell someone you trust how you actually feel
option_c: Fake being ill on the day
option_d: Tell everyone you think sports day is rubbish
explanation: Keeping a big feeling to yourself makes it heavier. This works the same way before a school trip, a performance, a match, starting a new club. Telling one person you trust makes it lighter and you might get support you did not expect.
hint: What happens to a big feeling when you keep it completely to yourself?

question: You worked really hard on your art project. The teacher says it is good but not brilliant. You feel crushed even though nobody was mean. Why might you feel that way?
option_a: Because you are too sensitive
option_b: Because you cared about it â€” and when something matters feedback hits harder
option_c: Because art does not count
option_d: Because the teacher is wrong
explanation: This happens with anything you put real effort into â€” a story, a goal, a project. The more you cared the more the feedback lands. That is not weakness. It means you tried properly.
hint: Does caring about something change how feedback feels?

question: You told your friend a secret and now you are pretty sure they told someone else. You feel sick about it but have no proof. What do you do with that feeling right now?
option_a: Ignore it â€” you might be wrong
option_b: Notice it is there, do not act on it yet, and keep your eyes open
option_c: Tell everyone your friend cannot be trusted
option_d: Confront your friend in front of the group
explanation: That sick feeling when trust might have been broken comes up in lots of situations â€” when something private gets around school, when someone's reaction tells you more than their words. Acting before you have proof usually makes things messier. Noticing and waiting is harder but smarter.
hint: Is a feeling proof of something or just a signal worth watching?

question: Everyone in your group laughed at something. You did not find it funny â€” you actually felt uncomfortable. But you laughed anyway. Why do people do that?
option_a: Because they are liars
option_b: Because fitting in feels safer than being the only one who does not laugh
option_c: Because it was probably funny and they did not realise
option_d: Because they are trying to impress someone
explanation: This happens in classrooms, on sports teams, at family gatherings. Laughing along when you do not mean it is something almost everyone does. Noticing when you are doing it is the first step to choosing something different next time.
hint: Is there a difference between laughing because something is funny and laughing for another reason?

question: Callum got tuna again. He looks like he might actually cry. Joel has pizza. Callum wants to swap. Joel does not want to but everyone at the table is telling him to. What should Joel do?
option_a: Give up the pizza because everyone is saying to
option_b: Pretend he cannot hear and eat the pizza very fast
option_c: Offer to swap half so they both get something decent
option_d: Say no and deal with Callum's sad face
explanation: This kind of pressure comes up everywhere â€” who picks the film, who gets the last seat, who has to go in goal. Going along just because people are loud is not the same as agreeing. Finding a middle usually works better for everyone.
hint: Does the loudest opinion have to win?

question: Your group has decided to give someone the silent treatment. You do not think it is fair but everyone else is doing it. What do you do?
option_a: Join in â€” you do not want it to be you next
option_b: Stay friendly with that person even if the group notices
option_c: Tell the teacher straight away
option_d: Tell the person being ignored what the group is doing
explanation: Groups can freeze someone out at school, in a sports team, in a club. Going along because everyone else does feels easier. But staying decent to someone being ignored does not make you the enemy â€” it makes you the one person actually doing the right thing.
hint: Is doing the right thing harder when everyone else is doing something different?

question: You said you did not want to join in with something. Now two people are saying come on, do not be boring, everyone else is doing it. What is probably happening?
option_a: They are right and you are being boring
option_b: The group is uncomfortable with one person thinking differently and trying to close that gap
option_c: They are being mean on purpose
option_d: You should just go along with it this time
explanation: This happens on the playground, in the street after school, in any group where one person says no and the rest keep pushing. The pushing feels like proof they are right. It is not. When a group cannot handle one person thinking differently they are usually not as sure as they look.
hint: Is being pushed to agree the same as being wrong?

question: You went along with the group even though you knew it was a bad idea. It went wrong exactly like you thought it would. Someone asks whose idea it was. What do you do?
option_a: Say it was not your idea since you never wanted to do it
option_b: Stay quiet and let someone else speak
option_c: Say honestly that you had doubts but went along with it anyway
option_d: Blame the person who started it
explanation: This comes up in group projects, in dares, in plans that go wrong on the way home. Being honest that you went along even when you had doubts is harder than blaming someone else â€” but it is the thing that builds real trust.
hint: What is the honest thing to say even when it is uncomfortable?

question: You said you needed more time to think. Now someone is saying you always do this and why can you never just go with things. How do you answer?
option_a: Say sorry and agree with them
option_b: Say nothing and go quiet
option_c: Say calmly that you just want to make sure it is a good idea before you say yes
option_d: Walk away from the group
explanation: This follow-up pressure happens in classrooms, friendship groups, sports teams â€” anywhere one person slows down to think and the group gets impatient. Always and never are words used to make you feel like the problem. One calm sentence is enough. You are thinking. That is allowed.
hint: Does someone saying you always do something make it true?

question: You told the group you did not want to climb through the building site fence and walked off. Now on Monday someone says you were scared. What do you do?
option_a: Explain to everyone why it was dangerous
option_b: Say nothing â€” people who know you will know it is not true
option_c: Agree that yes you were scared just to end it
option_d: Tell a teacher what they are saying
explanation: This happens after any moment where you made a sensible choice and someone wants to make it look weak. You made a good decision. Someone putting a label on it does not change that. The people who know you already know the truth.
hint: Does a label someone gives you have to stick?

question: You stayed friendly with the person the group was ignoring. Now someone is saying you are taking sides. Are you?
option_a: Yes â€” being friendly with someone is taking sides
option_b: No â€” being decent to someone is not the same as taking sides against everyone else
option_c: Maybe â€” you should have thought about that before
option_d: Yes â€” and you should apologise to the group
explanation: Being kind to one person does not mean you are against everyone else. Anyone saying it does is trying to force you to choose. In most cases you do not have to â€” and the person saying it knows that too.
hint: Is being kind to someone the same as being against everyone else?

question: Last week the Primary 7s kicked your group off the far patch mid-game. Today everyone wants to play there again. Mia says are we sure about that? Everyone tells her to stop worrying. You are the one the group listens to. What do you do?
option_a: Go with the majority â€” most people want the far patch
option_b: Say look it happened last week â€” let's just use the other side today
option_c: Tell Mia she needs to just go with it
option_d: Say nothing â€” it is not your job
explanation: This happens in any group heading somewhere that caused a problem before. You were there. You know. Saying it simply and offering a direction forward is all it takes. The group follows because you moved first.
hint: Does remembering what happened before help make a better decision now?

question: Your group is being horrible about someone who is not there. It is getting a bit much. Everyone is laughing. What do you do?
option_a: Join in â€” it is just a laugh
option_b: Go quiet but say nothing
option_c: Say something like alright that is enough and change the subject
option_d: Tell the group they are being horrible
explanation: This comes up in classrooms, group chats, changing rooms after PE, walking home. You do not need a speech. Saying alright that is enough and moving the conversation on is quick, not preachy, and it changes the energy in the room.
hint: Does changing the subject need a big explanation?

question: You suggested something for the group project. Jamie said nah boring and moved on. Two minutes later Jamie said basically the same thing and everyone loved it. You are the one people listen to. What do you do?
option_a: Say nothing â€” at least it is a good idea
option_b: Say out loud hang on â€” did they not just say that?
option_c: Tell Jamie off after the lesson
option_d: Vote for a different idea just to make a point
explanation: This happens in group projects, team meetings, any group where one voice gets heard more. You have the influence to name what just happened without turning it into a fight. Saying it simply means the right person gets the credit.
hint: Is it fair to let something like that go without saying anything?

question: Your group is about to do something you are pretty sure will get everyone in trouble. You can see it clearly. Nobody else seems bothered. What is the hardest but best thing to do?
option_a: Go along with it â€” if everyone gets in trouble at least you are together
option_b: Say nothing but do not join in
option_c: Say clearly this is going to go wrong and suggest something else
option_d: Wait until it goes wrong then say you knew it would
explanation: This comes up on the way home from school, in group dares, in plans that seem fine to everyone else but you can already see where they lead. Offering something else at the same time as saying it is wrong makes it easier for the group to turn around without feeling embarrassed.
hint: Is it better to say something before it goes wrong or after?

question: You said the group's plan was going to go wrong. It did not â€” it worked out fine. Now someone says you are always so negative. How do you handle that?
option_a: Apologise for saying it
option_b: Say you are glad it worked out and leave it there
option_c: Explain all the reasons you thought it would go wrong
option_d: Say well it might have gone wrong
explanation: This happens any time you raise a concern and it turns out fine. You were not wrong to say it. It is good that it worked out. Being genuinely glad about that and moving on is more powerful than defending yourself.
hint: Do you need to win an argument to have been right to say something?

question: You changed the group's direction and it worked. Now the person who wanted to do it the original way is being off with you. What do you do?
option_a: Apologise even though the decision was right
option_b: Give them a bit of space and check in with them later
option_c: Tell the group what they are doing
option_d: Be extra friendly so they have to come round
explanation: This comes up on sports teams, in friendship groups, anywhere your decision went against what someone else wanted. Someone being off when a decision went against them is normal. A bit of space and a quiet check-in later is nearly always enough.
hint: Does a good decision mean everyone will be happy straight away?

question: You are chosen to read out loud in class. Your voice goes wobbly even though you know the words perfectly. What is happening?
option_a: You are bad at reading
option_b: Your body got a surprise and is catching up â€” it happens to everyone
option_c: You should have practised more
option_d: You are showing off
explanation: This happens in class, at a sports presentation, reading something out at assembly. Your voice going wobbly when people are watching is your body reacting to being seen. It is not about how well you can read. It settles down the more you do it.
hint: Is your wobbly voice about reading or about being watched?

question: You fell out with your best friend on Monday. By Wednesday you have both sort of moved on but something still feels a bit off. What is that feeling?
option_a: Proof the friendship is over
option_b: Your feelings catching up with what your head already decided â€” they take a bit longer
option_c: Nothing â€” just ignore it
option_d: A sign you should bring the argument back up
explanation: This leftover feeling happens after any kind of falling out â€” with a friend, on your team, even after a telling off that has been sorted. Your head moves on first and your feelings follow a bit later. The feeling usually fades on its own.
hint: Can your head and your feelings be in different places at the same time?

question: Something happened at home this morning and now you are at school and you cannot concentrate. You feel like you are watching the lesson through glass. What should you do?
option_a: Push through and pretend everything is fine
option_b: Tell a trusted adult you are having a hard morning
option_c: Cause a distraction so nobody notices
option_d: Go to the toilet and stay there
explanation: Things from outside follow us in â€” to school, to training, to anywhere we are supposed to be focused. You do not have to explain everything. Telling one trusted person you are having a hard morning takes ten seconds and means someone knows â€” which usually makes the rest of the day easier.
hint: Does something that happened outside school stay outside when you walk in?

question: Everyone in your group picks the same answer on a quiz. You picked something different. You are now not sure if you are right or just different. What do you do?
option_a: Change your answer to match the group
option_b: Keep your answer â€” you picked it for a reason
option_c: Ask the teacher to check before you decide
option_d: Cross out your answer so nobody sees
explanation: This happens on quizzes, in group decisions, any time one person sees something differently from everyone else. Everyone else picking the same thing does not make it right. Keep your answer until you actually know it is wrong â€” not just because you are outnumbered.
hint: Is everyone picking the same thing the same as everyone being right?

question: Your group is laughing at the way someone in another group answered a question. You thought the answer was actually pretty good. What do you do?
option_a: Laugh along so you fit in
option_b: Stay quiet and keep your face straight
option_c: Say actually I thought that was a decent answer
option_d: Ask why everyone is laughing
explanation: This comes up in classrooms, watching someone perform, seeing someone try something new. One short sentence â€” actually I thought that was decent â€” is enough to stop a pile-on. Chances are you are not the only one in the group thinking it.
hint: Does saying what you actually think always have to be a big deal?

question: You kept your different answer on the quiz. You were right. Now the people who copied each other got it wrong. Someone says you only got it right by luck. What do you say?
option_a: Say nothing â€” being right is enough
option_b: Say maybe â€” but I kept my answer because I thought it through
option_c: Explain in detail how you worked it out
option_d: Say well you should have thought for yourself then
explanation: This comes after any moment where you went your own way and it paid off. You do not need to prove anything. Saying simply that you thought it through is calm and true. The result already did the talking.
hint: Do you need to prove yourself when the result already shows it?

question: Your group cannot agree on what game to play. Everyone is getting a bit grumpy. Nobody is deciding. You have got an idea. What do you do?
option_a: Wait for someone else to decide
option_b: Say right â€” what about this and suggest something specific
option_c: Take a vote and make it official
option_d: Give up and play on your own
explanation: Groups get stuck like this on the playground, on sports teams, in any situation where nobody wants to go first. When a group is going in circles one person saying something specific moves things forward. That is not being bossy â€” it is being useful.
hint: Is suggesting something the same as taking over?

question: You got picked for the team but your friend did not. You feel guilty even though it is not your fault. Is that feeling making sense?
option_a: No â€” you did nothing wrong so you should not feel anything
option_b: Yes â€” feeling bad when something good happens to you but not your friend is completely normal
option_c: No â€” you should just feel happy
option_d: Yes â€” you should tell the teacher to swap you out
explanation: This comes up whenever luck splits a group â€” one person gets picked and another does not, one person gets invited and another does not. Feeling guilty when something goes your way and not your friend's means you care about them. You do not need to fix it â€” just let it be there.
hint: Can you feel two things at the same time about the same moment?

question: Someone in your group has been left out all week. Nobody has said anything but everyone knows. The group keeps pretending it is not happening. What do you do?
option_a: Keep pretending along with everyone else
option_b: Go and sit with that person at lunch without making a big deal of it
option_c: Bring it up in front of the whole group
option_d: Tell a teacher everything
explanation: This happens in classrooms, on sports teams, in any group where someone has quietly been pushed to the edge. Going and sitting with someone without making a performance of it is the most useful single thing you can do and does not require anyone else to do anything.
hint: Does fixing a big situation always need a big action?

question: You can see the group is leaving someone out and it is getting worse every day. You are the one people follow. What one thing would change it fastest?
option_a: Have a serious talk with the group about being kind
option_b: Just include that person yourself â€” the group will follow your lead
option_c: Write a note to the teacher
option_d: Wait â€” it will sort itself out
explanation: This works the same way in any group. When the person the group looks to includes someone, the group adjusts around that without needing a big conversation. You do not need a speech. You just need to do it once and mean it.
hint: Does the person the group follows need to say something or just do something?

question: You stuck up for someone the group was leaving out. Now someone says you only did it to look good. What do you say?
option_a: Explain all your reasons for doing it
option_b: Say nothing â€” you know why you did it
option_c: Say maybe â€” but they still needed someone to sit with them
option_d: Ask why it matters why you did it
explanation: This challenge comes after any moment where you did something decent and someone questions your reason. Whether you did it to look good or not the person still needed someone. Saying so calmly ends the conversation. The reason does not change what actually happened.
hint: Does the reason for doing something kind change whether it was the right thing to do?

question: You are waiting to go on stage for the school show. Your tummy feels like it has butterflies. What is your body doing?
option_a: Telling you that you are going to be terrible
option_b: Getting you ready â€” that feeling is energy your body is making for you
option_c: Making you ill
option_d: Telling you to go home
explanation: That butterfly feeling before something important happens to almost everyone â€” before a match, a performance, a test, meeting someone new. Your body is not against you. It is making extra energy and focus. That is actually useful.
hint: Is the butterfly feeling your body helping you or worrying about you?

question: You are in a bad mood but you do not really know why. Your friend asks if you are ok and you snap at them. What is happening?
option_a: You are just a grumpy person
option_b: Your mood came out sideways at the wrong person at the wrong moment
option_c: Your friend annoyed you
option_d: You need to eat something
explanation: This happens to everyone â€” a mood that has no clear reason comes out at whoever is nearby. Knowing it happened means you can say sorry quickly and not let it turn into something bigger. The mood and the snap are two separate things.
hint: Can a mood that comes from nowhere still affect people around you?

question: Your team just lost a match because of a mistake someone else made. You really want to say something. What should you do first?
option_a: Say it straight away so they know how you feel
option_b: Take a breath and wait a few minutes before you say anything
option_c: Walk off without saying anything at all
option_d: Tell the coach what they did wrong
explanation: This moment comes up in matches, in group work, in any team situation where something goes wrong because of someone else. The few seconds between the feeling and the words are where the best choices live. Waiting even a little usually means what you say comes out much better.
hint: What happens to what you want to say if you wait just a little bit first?

question: Someone in your class says something that is not true about you in front of your friends. You feel furious. What is the best first move?
option_a: Say something sharp back straight away
option_b: Take yourself out of the moment for a few seconds before you decide what to do
option_c: Say nothing and pretend it did not happen
option_d: Tell a teacher immediately
explanation: Being put on the spot when something unfair is said feels enormous in that second. Taking yourself out of the moment â€” even just turning away, taking a breath â€” means you choose your response rather than just reacting. Reacting immediately almost always makes it bigger.
hint: Is the first thing you want to say always the best thing to say?

question: You are about to send a reply to a message that you wrote when you were really annoyed. Before you send it what should you do?
option_a: Send it â€” they need to know how you feel right now
option_b: Read it back once and ask yourself if you would still send it in an hour
option_c: Ask a friend if it sounds ok
option_d: Delete it and say nothing
explanation: Messages sent in anger are very hard to take back â€” in texts, in group chats, in any written message. Reading it back and asking if you would still send it in an hour is one of the most useful habits there is. Sometimes the answer is yes. Often it is not.
hint: Would you say the same thing in a different mood?

question: You know something about someone that would really change how your friend group sees them. You are tempted to share it. Before you do, what is the most important question to ask yourself?
option_a: Is it interesting enough to be worth sharing?
option_b: Is it actually mine to share?
option_c: Will it make people like me more?
option_d: Is it definitely true?
explanation: This comes up with secrets, with things overheard, with information that was shared with you privately. Information that was given to you in trust is not automatically yours to pass on. Asking whether it is yours to share is the question that stops the most damage.
hint: Just because you know something does not mean it is yours to share.

question: You have a big piece of homework due Friday. It is Monday. What is the smartest thing to do today?
option_a: Leave it until Thursday â€” you work better under pressure
option_b: Break it into small bits and do one bit each day
option_c: Do it all tonight and get it over with
option_d: Ask a friend to help you last minute
explanation: This works for any big task â€” a project, tidying a room, getting ready for something important. Doing a tiny bit each day means no single day feels too much. The whole thing gets done without one horrible evening.
hint: Does a big task have to be done all at once?

question: You have fifteen minutes before school and three things to do â€” find your PE kit, eat breakfast and brush your teeth. What order makes most sense?
option_a: Breakfast first because you are hungry
option_b: PE kit first because you do not know how long it will take
option_c: Teeth first because it is quickest
option_d: It does not matter â€” just do them in any order
explanation: When time is short do the unpredictable thing first. Finding a PE kit might take one minute or ten â€” you do not know. Do that first while you still have time. Then the rest is quick and predictable. This works any time you have a list of things and one of them might surprise you.
hint: Which task could take much longer than you expect?

question: Someone you do not know holds the door open for you. What do you do?
option_a: Walk through without saying anything â€” they chose to do it
option_b: Say thank you and make eye contact
option_c: Nod at them
option_d: Say something funny
explanation: This is one of those tiny moments that happens every day â€” doors, someone passing something, someone letting you go first. Saying thank you and making eye contact takes one second and it matters more than most people realise. It tells the other person they were seen.
hint: Does a small kind moment deserve to be noticed?

question: You are at a friend's house for dinner and you do not like the food. What do you do?
option_a: Tell them honestly it is not nice
option_b: Eat what you can and say thank you for dinner
option_c: Feed it to the family dog when nobody is looking
option_d: Ask for something different
explanation: Someone made that meal and thought about you when they did. Eating what you can and saying thank you honours the effort they made. This comes up at friends' houses, at family meals, at any time someone cooked for you. It is only one meal â€” and being a good guest is remembered.
hint: Who made the effort here and how does your reaction affect them?

question: You are talking with a friend and you suddenly remember something funny you want to say. They have not finished speaking yet. What should you do?
option_a: Jump in â€” your story is relevant
option_b: Wait for them to finish and then share it
option_c: Say sorry to interrupt but and jump in
option_d: Forget your story â€” it was probably not that good
explanation: This comes up in every conversation â€” at lunch, in class, with family. Waiting for someone to finish what they are saying before you speak tells them that what they are saying matters to you. It is one of the simplest and most noticed things about how someone talks to other people.
hint: Are you listening or just waiting for your turn?

question: You bump into someone in the corridor and knock their stuff on the floor. It was an accident. What do you do?
option_a: Keep walking â€” it was an accident so it does not really count
option_b: Say sorry and help pick it up
option_c: Say sorry quickly and keep walking
option_d: Look at them and wait to see if they say anything
explanation: Accidents happen â€” in corridors, at lunch, in the street. Saying sorry and helping straight away shows you care about how your actions affect someone even when you did not mean it. It takes about ten seconds and it matters.
hint: Does it being an accident change whether you should do something about it?

question: Your friend group has a habit of being quite loud and taking up a lot of space on the bus home. You notice other passengers looking uncomfortable. What do you do?
option_a: Nothing â€” you are allowed to be on the bus
option_b: Say to the group quietly let's bring it down a bit
option_c: Tell everyone individually to be quieter
option_d: Leave it â€” it is not your job to manage the group
explanation: Public spaces are shared â€” buses, libraries, shops, waiting rooms. Being aware of how much space your group takes up and saying something quietly about it is not being boring. It is noticing that other people are in the space too. One quiet word from the right person is usually enough.
hint: Does a shared space belong to your group or to everyone in it?

question: You have been on a screen for two hours. Your eyes feel tired and your neck aches. What should you do?
option_a: Keep going â€” you are in the middle of something
option_b: Take a break, look at something far away and move around
option_c: Switch to a different screen â€” that counts as a break
option_d: Have a snack and carry on
explanation: Your body sends clear signals when it needs a rest â€” tired eyes, stiff neck, headache. These happen during gaming, watching things, doing homework on a screen. The signals are worth listening to. A short break and some movement resets both your body and your brain.
hint: What is your body trying to tell you right now?

question: You skipped breakfast because you were running late. By mid-morning you cannot concentrate and everything is annoying you. What is probably happening?
option_a: You are just not a morning person
option_b: Your brain is running low on fuel and it is showing in your mood and focus
option_c: The lesson is boring
option_d: You did not sleep enough
explanation: Your brain runs on food. Skipping breakfast affects concentration and mood more than most people realise â€” in lessons, in training, in anything requiring focus. It is one of the most direct connections between what you do and how your brain works.
hint: What did your brain not get this morning that it needs to work?

question: You need to sleep but you keep checking your phone and you are not tired. The most useful thing to do is...
option_a: Keep going until you feel tired naturally
option_b: Put the phone in another room and do something calm instead
option_c: Turn the brightness down so it is less harmful
option_d: Watch something on TV instead
explanation: Phone screens tell your brain to stay awake even when you want to sleep. This affects how quickly you fall asleep and how well you sleep. Putting the phone in another room and doing something calm â€” reading, drawing, just lying quietly â€” lets your brain switch into sleep mode. This works on school nights, before matches, before any big day.
hint: What is the screen doing to your brain's sleep signals right now?

question: You are worrying about something happening tomorrow and you cannot stop thinking about it. What helps most?
option_a: Lie awake going over every possible thing that could go wrong
option_b: Write the worry down on paper and then put the paper away
option_c: Stay up late to distract yourself
option_d: Pretend you are not worried
explanation: Writing a worry down moves it from going round in your head onto paper â€” which often makes your brain feel like it has been recorded safely and it can stop repeating it. This works before tests, matches, performances, anything you are nervous about. The paper holds the worry so your head does not have to.
hint: What happens to a worry when you write it down instead of carrying it in your head?

question: After a really hard day you get home and just want to lie on your bed and do nothing for a while. Is that ok?
option_a: No â€” you should get your homework done while it is fresh
option_b: Yes â€” your brain and body sometimes need time to recover before you do anything else
option_c: No â€” lying around makes you feel worse
option_d: Yes â€” but only if you feel ill
explanation: After a demanding day your brain and body need time to come down before they are ready to do more. This is true after a difficult school day, a hard match, a stressful event. Giving yourself a short recovery time first usually means you do better at whatever comes next than if you push straight into it.
hint: Does your brain work the same at the end of a hard day as at the start?

question: You have been trying to read for ten minutes and your mind keeps wandering off to other things. What is the best response?
option_a: Give up â€” you clearly cannot focus today
option_b: Notice that your mind wandered, come back to the page and try again
option_c: Read faster so it is over sooner
option_d: Read the same sentence over and over until it goes in
explanation: Mind wandering during reading happens to everyone â€” in lessons, doing homework, reading anything that takes effort. The skill is noticing when your mind has drifted and bringing it back. Every single time you do that you are practising focus. The wandering is not the failure. Giving up would be.
hint: What should you do the moment you notice your mind has drifted?

question: You need to do your homework but your phone keeps buzzing with messages. What works best?
option_a: Check each message as it arrives then refocus
option_b: Put your phone in another room until the homework is done
option_c: Turn the sound off but keep it next to you
option_d: Check messages every five minutes as a short break
explanation: Every notification breaks your concentration â€” and it takes longer to get back into the work than the check took. This is true during homework, during revision, during anything requiring proper focus. Putting the phone out of reach removes the pull completely. Short focused work beats long distracted work every time.
hint: What happens to your focus every single time you check a message?

question: You walk into the classroom and everyone is sitting quietly while the teacher writes on the board. What should you do?
option_a: Say hello to your friends loudly so they know you are there
option_b: Sit down quietly and get your things out
option_c: Ask the teacher what they are writing
option_d: Start unpacking loudly
explanation: Reading the room means looking at what is happening around you and matching it. This works in classrooms, in someone's house, in any space where you are arriving into something already happening. The room tells you what it needs before anyone says a word.
hint: What is the room already telling you before anyone speaks?

question: You are telling a story and you notice two people have started looking away and one is checking their phone. What does this probably mean?
option_a: They are thinking carefully about what you are saying
option_b: Their attention has drifted and the story might need to end soon
option_c: They are being deliberately rude
option_d: They are all checking the same thing
explanation: When people look away or check their phones while you are talking it usually means their attention has moved on. This is information â€” not an attack. Noticing it means you can choose to wrap up, change direction or ask them something. Not noticing it means you keep going into a room that has already moved on.
hint: What are the people in front of you actually showing you right now?

question: You have brilliant news to share but your teacher looks very stressed and busy right now. What should you do?
option_a: Tell them immediately â€” your news is important
option_b: Wait until they look less rushed then share it
option_c: Say it loudly so they hear while they work
option_d: Give up â€” they probably will not care
explanation: Even good news lands badly when someone is not ready to hear it. This works in any situation â€” a parent who is on the phone, a friend who is upset, a teacher mid-task. Waiting for the right moment is not giving up. It is making sure the news actually gets heard properly.
hint: Is right now actually a good moment for the other person?

question: Your friend says they are fine but they have gone quiet, are not making eye contact and have stopped joining in. What does this probably mean?
option_a: They are fine â€” they said so
option_b: Something is probably wrong even though they said they are fine
option_c: They are tired
option_d: They are annoyed at you specifically
explanation: What people say and what their body shows are sometimes different. Going quiet, avoiding eye contact, pulling back â€” these are signals worth noticing. This comes up with friends, with family, with anyone you know well. Asking gently if they are actually ok is usually the right move.
hint: Are the words saying the same thing as everything else?

question: Something small happens â€” someone takes the seat you were heading for â€” and you feel a huge wave of anger that seems too big for what just happened. Why might that be?
option_a: You are just an angry person
option_b: That small thing probably set off a bigger feeling that was already there
option_c: You really needed that seat
option_d: You are having a bad reaction to something you ate
explanation: Big reactions to small things usually mean something bigger was already sitting underneath. This happens to everyone â€” a small thing tips a feeling that had been building. Knowing this means you can ask yourself what is actually going on rather than staying confused about why you reacted so strongly.
hint: Was the anger really about the seat?

question: You notice that when one person in your group is in a bad mood the whole group gets quieter and more careful. What does this tell you?
option_a: That person is just grumpy
option_b: One person's mood is affecting the whole group â€” that is worth noticing
option_c: The whole group must be tired
option_d: Everyone is polite
explanation: One person's mood can set the temperature for a whole group â€” in a classroom, on a team, in a family. Noticing this in your group means you understand something important about how groups work. It also means you can notice when your own mood might be doing the same thing to the people around you.
hint: Does one person's mood stay only with that person?

question: Your group is about to do something that would mean being unkind to someone else. Going along with it would mean fitting in. Not going along would mean standing out. What is the most honest question to ask yourself?
option_a: Will anyone find out if I join in?
option_b: Is this actually something I want to do or am I doing it to fit in?
option_c: Is it really that bad?
option_d: What would happen if I just watched?
explanation: This moment comes up in schools, in groups, anywhere fitting in and doing the right thing are pulling in different directions. Asking honestly whether you want to do it or whether you are doing it to fit in is the question that tells you most about who you actually are.
hint: Would you do this if nobody was watching?

question: You want to get better at something â€” drawing, football, reading, anything. What is the most useful thing to do first?
option_a: Wait until you feel ready to start
option_b: Pick one small thing to practise and do it a little bit every day
option_c: Try to do loads of it all at once to catch up quickly
option_d: Ask someone better than you to show you everything
explanation: Getting better at anything works the same way â€” a sport, an instrument, a school subject, a skill. A small amount done regularly beats a large amount done once. Your brain learns better through repeated short sessions than through one long one.
hint: Does getting better at something happen all at once or a bit at a time?

question: You set yourself a goal and you were doing well. Then you had one bad week and did nothing. What should you do?
option_a: Give up â€” you already broke the streak
option_b: Start a completely new goal instead
option_c: Pick up where you left off â€” one bad week does not end a goal
option_d: Do double the amount this week to catch up
explanation: Missing a week does not end a goal. This is true for reading habits, exercise, practice, anything you are trying to build. What matters is the pattern over time â€” not whether every single day was perfect. The most important step after a gap is just starting again without making a big deal of the gap.
hint: Is one bad week the same as failing?

question: You get something wrong in class and feel embarrassed. What is the most useful way to think about it?
option_a: Getting it wrong means you are not good at that thing
option_b: Getting it wrong is information â€” your brain just found out where it needs to work next
option_c: You should have tried harder
option_d: Everyone is judging you for it
explanation: Getting things wrong is how brains learn â€” in class, on a pitch, in practice, anywhere. The embarrassment is real and it passes quickly. The wrong answer is actually more useful to your brain than a right one because it shows exactly where to focus next.
hint: What does your brain do with a wrong answer that it cannot do with a right one?

question: You have a big goal but every time you think about it you feel overwhelmed and do nothing. What usually helps most?
option_a: Wait until you feel less overwhelmed
option_b: Break it into the smallest steps you can imagine and do just the first one
option_c: Tell yourself it is not that important
option_d: Ask someone else to help you plan it
explanation: Feeling overwhelmed by a big goal is almost always about seeing the whole thing at once rather than just the next step. This works for projects, for big changes, for anything that feels too large. The only step that matters right now is the first one. The rest can wait until that one is done.
hint: Do you need to see the whole path or just the next step?

question: A younger child at school is sitting alone and looks upset. You do not know them. What is the kindest thing to do?
option_a: Walk past â€” it is none of your business
option_b: Go over quietly and ask if they are ok
option_c: Stare at them
option_d: Tell your friends about them
explanation: Noticing when someone needs something and doing something small about it does not require knowing them. This comes up anywhere â€” a younger child at school, someone on a bus, a person in a waiting room. You do not have to fix anything. Just asking if they are ok can be the whole thing.
hint: Do you have to know someone to be kind to them?

question: Your friend got picked for something you both wanted. You feel happy for them but also disappointed for yourself. Is it ok to feel both at the same time?
option_a: No â€” if you are a good friend you should only feel happy for them
option_b: Yes â€” feeling two things at once is completely normal
option_c: No â€” the disappointment means you are jealous
option_d: Yes â€” but you should not let your friend see the disappointment
explanation: Feeling happy for someone and disappointed for yourself at the same moment is one of the most common human feelings there is. Both feelings are true at the same time and neither cancels the other out. Being honest about this to yourself â€” even if you do not say it out loud â€” is much better than pretending one of them is not there.
hint: Can two feelings that seem opposite both be true at the same time?

question: Someone gives you a compliment right before asking you for a favour. Something feels slightly off about the compliment. Should you notice that feeling?
option_a: No â€” a compliment is always nice so just enjoy it
option_b: Yes â€” your instinct that something feels off is worth paying attention to
option_c: No â€” you are being paranoid
option_d: Yes â€” and you should refuse whatever they ask
explanation: Noticing when something feels slightly off â€” even something that looks positive â€” is a useful skill. Compliments can be genuine and they can also be used to soften someone up before a request. Noticing the feeling does not mean assuming the worst. It just means paying attention to what comes next.
hint: What comes after the compliment?

question: Someone in your group is very kind to you â€” always there, always helping. But every time you try to do something on your own they seem a bit hurt or jump in to take over. What is worth noticing about this pattern?
option_a: They are just a very kind person
option_b: Their helpfulness might be about keeping you needing them as much as it is about helping you
option_c: They do not trust you to manage on your own
option_d: You should feel grateful rather than suspicious
explanation: Kindness that makes you less independent rather than more is worth looking at carefully. This comes up in friendships, in family, in any relationship. You can appreciate the care someone offers while also noticing whether it builds you up or keeps you reliant. Both things can be true.
hint: Does real help make you more capable or more dependent?

question: You are telling your friend something important and they say the right things but keep glancing at their phone. What is probably happening?
option_a: They are listening carefully
option_b: Part of their attention is somewhere else even though they are saying the right words
option_c: They are waiting for an important message
option_d: They find what you are saying boring
explanation: What someone says and where their attention actually is are sometimes different things. Glancing at a phone, half-turned away, eyes moving â€” these show you where someone's focus really is. Noticing this does not mean getting upset. It might just mean this is not the right moment for that conversation.
hint: Where is their attention actually going?

question: You think your teacher made a mistake in marking your work. You believe your answer was right. What is the best way to bring it up?
option_a: Say nothing â€” teachers are always right
option_b: Bring it up quietly after class and explain your thinking calmly
option_c: Argue about it in front of the class
option_d: Write a note and leave it on their desk
explanation: Disagreeing with someone in authority â€” a teacher, a coach, a parent â€” is sometimes completely valid. How you do it matters as much as what you say. Calmly, privately, with your reasoning ready gives you the best chance of being heard. In front of everyone usually just creates a situation rather than solving one.
hint: Is there a way to disagree that makes it more likely you will be heard?

question: You are in a conversation and you realise you have been talking about yourself for quite a long time. What should you do?
option_a: Keep going â€” they would say something if they were bored
option_b: Ask them something about themselves and actually listen to the answer
option_c: Say anyway and keep talking
option_d: Apologise and go quiet
explanation: Good conversations go back and forth. Noticing that you have been going for a while and asking about the other person is a simple and very noticed thing to do. This works with friends, with family, with anyone you are talking to. It tells them you are interested in more than just your own story.
hint: Is a good conversation mostly about talking or about balance?

question: You wake up late and everything feels rushed and stressful. Which one habit would make the biggest difference to your mornings?
option_a: Going to bed later so you are not as tired when you wake up
option_b: Getting your bag ready and clothes out the night before
option_c: Setting three alarms instead of one
option_d: Eating breakfast faster
explanation: Almost all morning stress comes from decisions that could have been made the night before. Bag packed, clothes ready, tomorrow checked â€” doing these things before bed means the morning is just carrying out a plan rather than making panicked decisions in a rush.
hint: When is the best time to prepare for tomorrow?

question: You have been sitting doing homework for an hour and you feel foggy and restless. What does your body need?
option_a: More time â€” the foggy feeling will pass if you push through
option_b: To move â€” even five minutes of movement resets your brain and body
option_c: A snack so you have more energy to concentrate
option_d: To stop for the day
explanation: Your brain and body are not designed to stay still for long periods. The foggy restless feeling after a long sit is your body asking to move. Five minutes of actual movement â€” walking, stretching, going outside â€” resets both brain and body more effectively than any other short break.
hint: What does restless actually mean when your body says it?

question: You are having a great day when a friend says something that upsets you. The good feeling disappears quickly. Is that normal?
option_a: No â€” if your day was good one comment should not change it
option_b: Yes â€” feelings can shift quickly and that is completely normal
option_c: No â€” it means you are too sensitive
option_d: Yes â€” but it means you should be more careful about who you spend time with
explanation: Feelings change quickly â€” a good mood shifted by one moment, a bad morning turning around after one kind thing. This is normal and it works both ways. It does not mean you are too sensitive. It means you are paying attention to what is happening around you.
hint: Can feelings change quickly without something being wrong?

question: You have a decision to make and everyone is telling you what to do. You feel pulled in different directions. What is the most useful thing to do first?
option_a: Go with the majority â€” most people cannot be wrong
option_b: Go with whoever you trust most
option_c: Stop and ask yourself what you actually think before listening to anyone else
option_d: Wait until someone gives you a reason you cannot argue with
explanation: When everyone is giving you their version of what you should do it can drown out your own thinking. Stopping and asking yourself what you actually think â€” before all the other voices â€” means your decision has your own reasoning in it rather than just the loudest person's. Then you can listen to others with your own position already in place.
hint: What do you actually think before you hear what anyone else says?

question: You notice that the same person keeps being interrupted every time they speak in group discussions â€” by the same few people. Nobody else seems to have noticed. What do you do?
option_a: Nothing â€” group discussions are just like that
option_b: Next time it happens say hang on let them finish
option_c: Tell the teacher what you noticed
option_d: Ask the interrupted person if they have noticed
explanation: Patterns in groups â€” who gets heard, who gets talked over, who gets ignored â€” are often invisible to most people in the group. Noticing them is a skill. Doing one small thing about it â€” saying hang on let them finish â€” is often enough to change the pattern without making it a big issue.
hint: Does noticing something mean you have to make a big deal of it to do something about it?

question: You did something you knew was wrong and now you feel bad about it even though nobody found out. What is that feeling?
option_a: Nothing important â€” if nobody knows it does not matter
option_b: Guilt â€” and it is your brain telling you something needs to be made right
option_c: Just embarrassment about getting caught
option_d: A sign you need to forget about it and move on
explanation: Guilt is the feeling your brain gives you when it knows something is not right â€” even if nobody else knows. It feels bad on purpose. That uncomfortable feeling is your own sense of right and wrong working correctly. It is one of the most useful feelings you have.
hint: Why does guilt feel bad even when nobody found out?

question: Your best friend asks you what you think of something they made and you do not think it is very good. What do you do?
option_a: Tell them it is brilliant so they feel good
option_b: Say nothing useful
option_c: Find one thing you genuinely like and say that honestly alongside one honest thought about what could be better
option_d: Tell them exactly everything you think is wrong with it
explanation: Being honest with someone you care about does not mean saying everything you think. Finding something genuine to say first and then one honest thought about what could improve is kind and useful. This works with friends, with family, with teammates. Pure flattery helps nobody. Pure criticism damages things. The middle way is the skill.
hint: Can you be kind and honest at the same time?

question: You tried something new and were not very good at it the first time. What does that actually mean?
option_a: That you are not suited to that thing
option_b: That you are at the beginning â€” nobody is good at something new the first time
option_c: That you should try a different version
option_d: That you need someone to show you again
explanation: Being new at something and not being good at it yet are the same thing. This is true for any skill â€” a sport, an instrument, a subject, a social situation. The first time is supposed to be difficult. That difficulty is not a verdict. It is just the start.
hint: Is not being good at something new the same as not being able to do it?

question: Someone you do not know very well seems to be having a really bad day. They have not said anything to you. What is the kindest thing you could do?
option_a: Leave them alone â€” they will ask for help if they need it
option_b: Say something simple like are you alright without making a big deal of it
option_c: Tell a friend about it
option_d: Ignore it â€” you do not know them well enough
explanation: You do not have to know someone well to notice they are struggling and say something. A simple are you alright without pressure or a big fuss is often all it takes. This works in school, on a team, anywhere. Most people who are having a hard time are not going to ask for help â€” but they will notice someone who saw it.
hint: Do you have to know someone well to notice they need something?

question: You were part of a group that did something that got someone else in trouble for something they did not do. Nobody is saying anything. What should you do?
option_a: Say nothing â€” what happened happened
option_b: Say what actually happened even though it is uncomfortable
option_c: Wait and see if the other person gets it sorted out themselves
option_d: Privately tell the person who got in trouble that you know
explanation: Staying quiet when someone else gets blamed for something you were part of is one of the hardest situations to navigate honestly. But the discomfort of speaking up is always less than the discomfort of knowing you did not. This is true at school, on teams, in any group situation.
hint: Is saying nothing the same as doing nothing wrong?

question: You are really excited about something but everyone around you seems calm and is not that interested. You feel a bit deflated. Why?
option_a: Your excitement was probably wrong
option_b: Excitement that is not matched by the room can feel lonely â€” and that feeling is normal
option_c: You were too enthusiastic
option_d: You should have kept it to yourself
explanation: Excitement that is not met by the room is one of the most deflating feelings there is. It does not mean your excitement was wrong or too much. It just means the people around you were not in the same place at that moment. That mismatch is uncomfortable and very common.
hint: Does excitement need to be matched by the room to be real?

question: You are in a situation that is already tense. You feel the urge to say something sharp or funny to show you are not bothered. Before you say it, what is worth asking yourself?
option_a: Is it actually funny enough?
option_b: Will this make things better or am I just trying to look like I am not affected?
option_c: Will people think I am cool for saying it?
option_d: Is it true?
explanation: Sharp comments in tense moments can feel like armour â€” they signal that you are fine and in control. But they often make things bigger rather than smaller. Asking whether you are doing it for the room or to protect yourself is the question that tells you whether it is actually a good idea.
hint: Are you saying it for the situation or for yourself?

question: A classmate answers a question wrong in class and two people snigger. You feel uncomfortable watching it. What could you do?
option_a: Nothing â€” it was just a small laugh
option_b: Keep your face still and do not join in
option_c: Laugh along so you do not seem like you are taking sides
option_d: Tell the teacher what the two people did
explanation: Not joining in when someone is being laughed at is harder than it sounds. You do not have to say anything or make it a moment. Just keeping your face straight and not laughing is enough to not be part of it â€” and sometimes it is noticed by the person who needed it.
hint: Does not doing something count as doing something?

question: After a really big argument everything goes quiet and you feel completely flat and empty. Why does that happen?
option_a: Because you lost the argument
option_b: Because big emotional moments use a lot of energy and your body needs to recover after them
option_c: Because something is wrong with you
option_d: Because the argument was not really resolved
explanation: Big emotional moments â€” arguments, performances, difficult conversations â€” use real physical energy. Feeling flat and empty afterward is your body coming down from that. It happens after exciting things as well as difficult ones. Rest is what it actually needs.
hint: What did that big moment cost your body?

question: Someone is telling you about a falling out they had with another friend. The story makes that friend sound completely at fault. Before you decide what to think, what is the most useful question to ask yourself?
option_a: Is this person usually honest?
option_b: Am I only hearing one side of what happened?
option_c: Did they deserve what happened to them?
option_d: Should I get involved?
explanation: Every story about a conflict is told from one point of view. The person telling it is not necessarily lying â€” they are just telling it from where they were standing. The person who is not there has their own version. Holding the information lightly until you have more of the picture is fairer to everyone â€” including the person telling you.
hint: How many sides does every falling out have?

question: You find a subject at school really difficult. Someone else seems to get it straight away. What is the most useful thing to tell yourself?
option_a: They are just smarter than me at this
option_b: They might have had more practice at it or found their way into it earlier â€” it does not mean I cannot get there
option_c: I will never be as good as them at this
option_d: I should stick to what I am already good at
explanation: Finding something harder than someone else does not mean a permanent gap. Almost every skill gap between two people comes from different amounts of practice and different entry points â€” not from one person being capable and the other not. This is true in every subject, every sport, every skill.
hint: Is someone finding something easier than you proof that you cannot do it?

question: You noticed something unfair happening to someone in your group. You want to say something but the moment passed and now it feels too late. Is it actually too late?
option_a: Yes â€” if you did not say it at the time it does not count now
option_b: No â€” saying something later is still better than saying nothing at all
option_c: Yes â€” bringing it up now will just cause more problems
option_d: No â€” but you should wait until the group is together to raise it
explanation: The right moment to say something is not always the moment it happens. Coming back to something later â€” telling someone quietly you noticed, checking in after, saying something when you have had time to think â€” is often better than a rushed reaction in the moment. Later is not too late.
hint: Is the only time to say something the exact moment it happens?

question: You have been getting to bed late all week and you are exhausted. You know what you need to do. What usually stops people actually doing it?
option_a: They do not know that sleep matters
option_b: They know but in the moment the screen or the conversation feels more important than tomorrow's tiredness
option_c: They are not tired enough yet
option_d: They will sort it out at the weekend
explanation: Knowing what helps and actually doing it are two very different things. The pull of a screen or a conversation in the moment is real and immediate. The tiredness is tomorrow. Understanding that gap â€” between knowing and doing â€” is how you start to close it.
hint: Is knowing what to do the same as doing it?

question: Someone in your class behaves in a way that everyone finds difficult â€” they are loud, they take over, they make things awkward. You feel annoyed. What else might be worth considering?
option_a: Nothing â€” their behaviour is their behaviour
option_b: That behaviour that takes up a lot of space usually comes from something going on for that person â€” it does not excuse it but it might explain it
option_c: That they just enjoy making things difficult
option_d: That they need to be told how annoying they are
explanation: People who take up too much space in a room â€” loudly, aggressively, by dominating â€” are almost always doing it because something is going on for them. Understanding that does not mean excusing the behaviour or putting up with it. It means you see the person more clearly than just their most difficult surface.
hint: Is difficult behaviour usually about the moment it happens in?

question: You have started acting quite differently around a new group of friends â€” louder, or quieter, or saying things you would not normally say. You notice it yourself. What is worth thinking about?
option_a: Nothing â€” everyone changes a little around different people
option_b: Whether the version of you that shows up around them is one you actually recognise and feel comfortable with
option_c: Whether they are having a bad effect on you
option_d: Whether your old friends would notice the difference
explanation: Adapting a little around different people is normal. But noticing a version of yourself that feels unfamiliar or uncomfortable is worth paying attention to. The question is not whether you change around people â€” you do, everyone does â€” but whether any version of you requires you to be someone you do not actually want to be.
hint: Is there a difference between adapting and becoming someone different?

question: You are at the cinema with your group. Something funny happens and your group gets quite loud for a while. People around you start looking over. What should you do?
option_a: Keep going â€” it was funny
option_b: Bring the noise down â€” other people are sharing the space
option_c: Tell the people looking to mind their own business
option_d: Leave the cinema
explanation: Public spaces are shared â€” cinemas, libraries, buses, waiting rooms, restaurants. How much noise and space your group takes up affects everyone else in the room. Bringing it down when people around you are affected is not about being boring. It is about noticing that other people are there too.
hint: Who else is in this space right now?

question: You are in a group and you notice one person has gone very quiet and seems to have pulled back from the conversation. Nobody else has noticed. What do you do?
option_a: Nothing â€” if they wanted to join in they would
option_b: Check in with them after â€” a quick are you ok is enough
option_c: Try to bring them into the conversation right now in front of everyone
option_d: Tell someone else in the group what you noticed
explanation: Noticing someone pulling back is a skill â€” and doing something about it quietly is another. Bringing someone back into a conversation in front of everyone can feel embarrassing for them. A quiet check-in after costs you almost nothing and it is almost always appreciated.
hint: Does helping someone have to happen in front of everyone to count?

question: You have done really well at something but instead of feeling proud you feel like you did not really deserve it or someone else could have done it better. Why does that happen to people?
option_a: Because they probably did not deserve it
option_b: Because people often find it hard to take in good things about themselves as easily as they take in bad things
option_c: Because they are being modest
option_d: Because they need more practice before they can feel confident
explanation: Finding it easier to believe negative things about yourself than positive ones is something a lot of people experience. A mistake lands and sticks. A success gets explained away. Noticing when you are doing this â€” and letting the good thing actually land â€” is something worth practising.
hint: Is it as easy to take in something good about yourself as something difficult?

question: Your friend group is drifting toward doing something that does not sit right with you. Nobody has said it directly but you can feel it building. What is the most useful thing to do?
option_a: Wait and see what happens
option_b: Name it to yourself clearly first â€” what exactly is it that does not feel right
option_c: Go along with it and decide later
option_d: Leave the group
explanation: Before you can respond to something that does not feel right you need to name it clearly to yourself â€” not just a vague feeling that something is off, but a specific thought about what it is. That naming is what gives you something to actually work with. It also helps you decide if and how to say something.
hint: Can you respond to something you have not named clearly yet?

question: You made a goal and told everyone about it. Now you are struggling and you do not want anyone to know. What is more useful â€” hiding it or being honest?
option_a: Hiding it â€” failing in private is better than failing in public
option_b: Being honest â€” saying it is harder than you thought usually gets you support rather than judgement
option_c: Giving up quietly so nobody notices
option_d: Pretending it is going fine
explanation: Telling someone a goal is difficult is almost always met with more support than judgement. People who have tried things know how hard it is. The fear that admitting struggle means failing is usually bigger than the actual reality of saying it out loud.
hint: Is admitting something is hard the same as giving up?

question: Someone did something kind for you a while ago and you never properly thanked them. It still feels a bit unfinished. What should you do?
option_a: Nothing â€” too much time has passed
option_b: Say thank you now â€” it is not too late and it will still mean something
option_c: Mention it to a mutual friend
option_d: Do something kind for them without explaining why
explanation: Thanking someone for something they did â€” even weeks later â€” almost always lands well. Most people do not expect to be thanked and so being thanked, even late, is noticed. It is not too late unless you decide it is.
hint: Is there a deadline on saying thank you?

question: You walk into a room and before anyone says a word you feel more relaxed near some people and more tense near others. What is that about?
option_a: You are imagining it
option_b: Your instincts are picking up very fast signals from how people hold themselves, their expression and how they respond to you â€” and you are reading them quickly
option_c: You already know who you like
option_d: You are just nervous about being in a new space
explanation: Your instincts read people quickly â€” posture, eye contact, expression, stillness or restlessness. This happens before anyone speaks. It is not imagination and it is not irrational. It is a very fast version of the same reading you do consciously when you get to know someone. Those first signals are usually worth paying attention to.
hint: What are you picking up before anyone says a word?

question: Your best friend is upset about something that you do not think is that big a deal. Should you tell them that?
option_a: Yes â€” being honest is better than pretending
option_b: No â€” what feels big to them is big to them even if it would not be big to you
option_c: Yes â€” they need to have some perspective
option_d: No â€” but you do not have to listen for too long
explanation: What is a big deal is different for different people. Telling someone their feelings are too big for the situation almost always makes them feel worse and less likely to talk to you next time. Listening without measuring their feelings against yours is one of the most useful things you can do for a friend.
hint: Do feelings have to make sense to someone else to be real?

question: Someone is being very nice to you but something about it feels slightly off. What should you do with that feeling?
option_a: Ignore it â€” they are being nice
option_b: Notice it and pay attention to what happens next
option_c: Tell them you think they want something
option_d: Avoid them
explanation: A feeling that something is slightly off â€” even when everything looks fine on the surface â€” is worth paying attention to. It does not mean assuming the worst. It means watching what happens next rather than dismissing the signal. Your instincts are often picking up something real before your thinking catches up.
hint: What is the feeling actually telling you to do?

question: Your group is stuck going round in circles on a decision and getting grumpy. Nobody is moving forward. You have an idea. What is the most useful thing to do?
option_a: Keep waiting â€” someone will decide eventually
option_b: Say right â€” what about this and give them something specific to respond to
option_c: Take a vote on every option
option_d: Tell the group they are going round in circles
explanation: When a group is stuck what it usually needs is one person to say something specific. Not a speech about how they are stuck â€” just a concrete idea that gives everyone something to react to. Moving things forward with a suggestion is nearly always more useful than talking about the fact that things are stuck.
hint: What does a group stuck in circles need more than anything else?

question: You can see that someone in your group is being quietly made to feel small over time â€” small comments, being left out of things, being talked over. Nobody is naming it. What do you do?
option_a: Wait â€” it might sort itself out
option_b: Do one thing that directly includes that person and shows the group they are valued â€” without making a speech
option_c: Tell the group what you have noticed in front of everyone
option_d: Report it to a teacher straight away
explanation: Patterns of quiet exclusion or belittling in a group rarely sort themselves out. The most effective thing the right person can do is include the affected person directly and clearly â€” not a speech, not a confrontation â€” just one unmistakable act of inclusion. The group reads that and usually adjusts.
hint: Does changing a group pattern need a big confrontation or a clear act?

question: You are about to walk into a difficult situation. You feel the nerves rising. What is the most useful thing to know about that feeling?
option_a: It means something will go wrong
option_b: It means you are not ready
option_c: It means something matters to you and your body is getting you ready â€” that energy is actually useful
option_d: It means you should avoid the situation
explanation: The nerves before something difficult or important are your body getting ready â€” making you more alert, more focused, more ready to respond. That feeling is not a warning to stop. It is preparation. Knowing the difference between your body preparing you and your body telling you something is actually wrong is one of the most useful things you can learn.
hint: Is the nervous feeling your body stopping you or getting you ready?

question: You are at your best friend's birthday party but you feel left out even though you were invited. What is that feeling telling you?
option_a: That you do not belong there
option_b: That something feels off and it is worth noticing
option_c: That you should go home
option_d: That you are being silly
explanation: This happens anywhere â€” at a party, walking into a new sports club, sitting in a classroom where everyone already knows each other. The feeling does not mean you do not belong. It just means something feels off and it is worth noticing.
hint: Does the feeling mean something is wrong or just that something feels different?

question: Your teacher reads out your gold star in front of the class. Everyone claps. You feel embarrassed instead of happy. Is that ok?
option_a: No â€” you should feel happy when you get praised
option_b: Yes â€” people feel things differently and embarrassment when being watched is very normal
option_c: No â€” it means you do not like your teacher
option_d: Yes â€” it means you did not deserve it
explanation: Being watched can feel uncomfortable even when something good is happening. This comes up at prize givings, sports presentations, being picked out in assembly. Your feelings are being honest â€” not wrong.
hint: Can a good moment feel uncomfortable at the same time?

question: Your whole class is excited about sports day. You are dreading it. You have not told anyone. What is the best thing to do with that feeling?
option_a: Pretend to be excited so nobody notices
option_b: Tell someone you trust how you actually feel
option_c: Fake being ill on the day
option_d: Tell everyone you think sports day is rubbish
explanation: Keeping a big feeling to yourself makes it heavier. This works the same way before a school trip, a performance, a match, starting a new club. Telling one person you trust makes it lighter and you might get support you did not expect.
hint: What happens to a big feeling when you keep it completely to yourself?

question: You worked really hard on your art project. The teacher says it is good but not brilliant. You feel crushed even though nobody was mean. Why might you feel that way?
option_a: Because you are too sensitive
option_b: Because you cared about it â€” and when something matters feedback hits harder
option_c: Because art does not count
option_d: Because the teacher is wrong
explanation: This happens with anything you put real effort into â€” a story, a goal, a project. The more you cared the more the feedback lands. That is not weakness. It means you tried properly.
hint: Does caring about something change how feedback feels?

question: You told your friend a secret and now you are pretty sure they told someone else. You feel sick about it but have no proof. What do you do with that feeling right now?
option_a: Ignore it â€” you might be wrong
option_b: Notice it is there, do not act on it yet, and keep your eyes open
option_c: Tell everyone your friend cannot be trusted
option_d: Confront your friend in front of the group
explanation: That sick feeling when trust might have been broken comes up in lots of situations â€” when something private gets around school, when someone's reaction tells you more than their words. Acting before you have proof usually makes things messier. Noticing and waiting is harder but smarter.
hint: Is a feeling proof of something or just a signal worth watching?

question: Everyone in your group laughed at something. You did not find it funny â€” you actually felt uncomfortable. But you laughed anyway. Why do people do that?
option_a: Because they are liars
option_b: Because fitting in feels safer than being the only one who does not laugh
option_c: Because it was probably funny and they did not realise
option_d: Because they are trying to impress someone
explanation: This happens in classrooms, on sports teams, at family gatherings. Laughing along when you do not mean it is something almost everyone does. Noticing when you are doing it is the first step to choosing something different next time.
hint: Is there a difference between laughing because something is funny and laughing for another reason?

question: Callum got tuna again. He looks like he might actually cry. Joel has pizza. Callum wants to swap. Joel does not want to but everyone at the table is telling him to. What should Joel do?
option_a: Give up the pizza because everyone is saying to
option_b: Pretend he cannot hear and eat the pizza very fast
option_c: Offer to swap half so they both get something decent
option_d: Say no and deal with Callum's sad face
explanation: This kind of pressure comes up everywhere â€” who picks the film, who gets the last seat, who has to go in goal. Going along just because people are loud is not the same as agreeing. Finding a middle usually works better for everyone.
hint: Does the loudest opinion have to win?

question: Your group has decided to give someone the silent treatment. You do not think it is fair but everyone else is doing it. What do you do?
option_a: Join in â€” you do not want it to be you next
option_b: Stay friendly with that person even if the group notices
option_c: Tell the teacher straight away
option_d: Tell the person being ignored what the group is doing
explanation: Groups can freeze someone out at school, in a sports team, in a club. Going along because everyone else does feels easier. But staying decent to someone being ignored does not make you the enemy â€” it makes you the one person actually doing the right thing.
hint: Is doing the right thing harder when everyone else is doing something different?

question: You said you did not want to join in with something. Now two people are saying come on, do not be boring, everyone else is doing it. What is probably happening?
option_a: They are right and you are being boring
option_b: The group is uncomfortable with one person thinking differently and trying to close that gap
option_c: They are being mean on purpose
option_d: You should just go along with it this time
